top10.introHtml: Ranked from 25 listings captured from AutoTrader (70 total in market). Cars.com and CarGurus required the Chrome extension for full data — both returned 0 accessible local listings this automated run. Rankings apply the cheap fun second car lens: top condition > reliability > fun factor > mileage.

picks.whyGood: The Z3 2.8 uses BMW's M52 straight-six — smoother and stronger than the 1.9 four-banger. At 75,000 miles and $7,940 with a clean title and no-accident history, this is the best dollar-per-smile on the list. Private seller means room to negotiate. Low miles suggest careful ownership; inline-six BMWs reward that care.
picks.whatToVerify: Operate soft top through a full cycle; check headliner and carpet for water intrusion at the rear window seam. Inspect rocker panels and floor jack points for rust — Z3s rust here in PA winters. Check cooling system (thermostats, hoses, expansion tank) — common weak point. Look for oil leaks at valve cover and rear main. Ask for service records; timing chain guides on M52 are a known wear item past 100K.
picks.bottomLine: Best buy on the board — low miles, clean title, right price, and the 2.8 straight-six makes it genuinely fun.

picks.whyGood: The MR2 Spyder (W30) is a mid-engine two-seat roadster with a Toyota 1ZZ-FE that regularly sees 200K+ miles. At 74,000 miles with no accidents and clean title, this is a screaming deal at $9,250. Mid-engine balance makes it one of the sharpest-handling cars under $15K anywhere. Most came with a 5-speed or the SMT sequential — either is engaging.
picks.whatToVerify: Confirm transmission type (5-speed manual is preferred; SMT can be balky). Operate soft top fully. Check for oil burning on 1ZZ — common above 100K but this car has 74K so low risk. Inspect for rear-end rust. Confirm no history of overheating (small coolant system). Check the convertible top hydraulics if SMT-equipped.
picks.bottomLine: The hidden gem — mid-engine Toyota reliability at roadster prices; if it checks out, buy it.

picks.whyGood: Newly listed 2011 C70 T5 at 74,000 miles from a franchise dealer (Keyser Miller Ford). The retractable hardtop gives full weather protection unlike a fabric soft-top; no accident history. At $8,995 this is well-priced for a C70 with this mileage. Turbo five-cylinder is strong and Volvo build quality ages well.
picks.whatToVerify: Cycle the retractable hardtop through open/close multiple times — hydraulic actuators and latch sensors are the C70's weak point; any hesitation or error code is a red flag. Check for CEL codes (common: ETM, throttle body). Inspect convertible top well for debris and drain blockage (causes water intrusion into trunk). Ask dealer for service history.
picks.bottomLine: Solid all-weather convertible with low miles at a fair price — cycle the roof mechanism carefully before committing.

picks.whyGood: E85 Z4 is sharper and more modern than the Z3 — better chassis, bigger cabin, improved ergonomics. The 2.5i straight-six is torquey and refined. At 88K miles with no accidents and a price-drop listing from a nearby Mainline dealer, this is well-positioned. Close at 16 miles means an easy PPI.
picks.whatToVerify: Soft top condition and operation — check for tears at the corners and clarity of rear plastic window. Z4 E85 is known for VANOS rattle on cold starts (normal wear but note severity). Check subframe for cracks — less common on 2.5i than M54 cars but worth a look. Oil leaks at valve cover. Cooling system condition.
picks.bottomLine: More modern than the Z3 with a price drop — a solid daily-driveable roadster at a fair ask.

picks.whyGood: E93 328i convertible with the N52B30 naturally-aspirated straight-six — smooth, reliable, and sounds great with the top down. 80K miles, clean title. The E93 hardtop convertible is well-engineered and weather-tight. At $10,499 from a private seller 15 miles away, this is attainably priced for a 4-seat droptop.
picks.whatToVerify: Cycle the retractable hardtop; listen for any hydraulic whine or pump hesitation. Check for water intrusion in trunk (common when top drain channels clog). Inspect subframe for cracks (E9x cars can crack near rear diff mounts — common on RWD BMWs). Cooling system: thermostat, expansion tank. VANOS and valve cover gasket are typical wear items.
picks.bottomLine: A 4-seat hardtop convertible with a great engine at a private-seller price — good value if the roof mechanism checks out.

picks.whyGood: B7 Audi A4 cabriolet at only 62,000 miles — the lowest mileage on this entire list. No accidents. The 2.0T quattro is a strong all-season convertible. At $11,500 the price is at the higher end but justified by the exceptional mileage. Selden Motors is a known local dealer.
picks.whatToVerify: Soft top: cycle fully, inspect fabric for UV fade/cracks, check rear window defroster function. 2.0T (BWT/BGB code) timing chain tensioner — critical: ask if it's been updated or replaced (known issue on 2005-2008 2.0T). PCV system for oil consumption. Quattro DSG/Tiptronic fluid service history. Check for water in the trunk via rear drain blockage.
picks.bottomLine: Lowest-mileage car on the list — 62K is extraordinary for a 2007; just verify the 2.0T timing chain tensioner.

picks.whyGood: A Porsche Boxster for $11,250 with 74K miles and no accidents is genuinely exciting — the 986 Boxster is one of the best-driving cars ever made at any price. Clean title. Only 11 miles away. If the IMS bearing has been addressed, this is the most rewarding car on the list by a wide margin.
picks.whatToVerify: IMS bearing is the make-or-break question: ask directly whether it has been replaced (LN Engineering retrofit or Porsche dealer update). If not documented, budget ~$2,000-3,000 for the fix at next clutch service and price accordingly. Operate soft top fully. Check for coolant leaks at rear (M96 engines). RMS (rear main seal) oil leak is common — check under the car. Intermediate shaft bearing document is critical.
picks.bottomLine: The most exciting car on this list — a real Porsche — but the IMS bearing answer determines everything; no documentation = walk away or reprice.

picks.whyGood: A second-gen R52 MINI Cooper S at 65K miles is surprisingly low for a 2007. The S trim adds the supercharged 1.6L (R52 gen is supercharged, not turbocharged) for genuine fun. No accidents. At $9,500 this is priced well for the mileage.
picks.whatToVerify: Confirm it's the R52 (2005-2008 soft-top convertible) not the R57 (turbocharged). Operate the soft top — check rear window and operation mechanism. R52 supercharger coupling can slip (whine at boost — listen carefully). Check transmission for shudder (R52 manual is preferred; auto has known issues). Cooling system: electric water pump and thermostat are R52 weak points.
picks.bottomLine: Low-mileage fun car at a fair price — the S trim earns its keep; just verify the supercharger coupling.

picks.whyGood: The NC Miata (2006-2015) is the definitive fun second car — perfectly balanced, direct steering, and the 2.0L goes well past 200K if maintained. Grand Touring adds Bose audio, leather, and the power-retractable hardtop (PRHT) on this trim. At 177K miles, the price of $11,450 demands inspection but Miata engines at this mileage are often still strong.
picks.whatToVerify: Timing chain service history (NC Miata uses a chain, not belt — but check it). Check for top rattles and seal condition; PRHT mechanism should cycle without hesitation. Inspect rocker panels for rust. Look for oil consumption or blue smoke. Verify no P0420 catalyst codes. Brakes and suspension bushings at 177K are likely overdue.
picks.bottomLine: High miles on the right car — the Miata earns this ranking purely on the strength of its platform; a compression test and PPI seal the deal.

picks.whyGood: R57 MINI at $7,999 is the most accessible entry on the list — 11 miles away, no accidents, and the R57 gen (2009-2015) is more reliable than the earlier R52. At 120K miles it's mid-life for a well-maintained MINI. CrossRoads is a local shop.
picks.whatToVerify: Soft top operation and condition — R57 tops are fabric; check for tears at corners and rear window clarity. Timing chain service (R57 N14/N18 engines had timing chain tensioner issues — confirm replacement). Check for coolant leaks and water pump condition. MINI electrics: scan for codes before committing.
picks.bottomLine: Budget pick at the right price — a no-accidents R57 at 11 miles away is a practical fun car if the timing chain is sorted.

mustangs.introHtml: 1 Mustang found in page-1 AutoTrader data. AutoTrader's make filter shows 5 total Fords in the 70-listing pool — additional Mustangs may be on pages 2+, inaccessible this run without Chrome.

picks.whyGood: A 1995 SN-95 Mustang GT with only 55,000 miles and no accidents is a remarkable find — this car has barely been used. The GT's 5.0L HO V8 (215 hp in 1995) delivers real muscle-car character. At $10,495 from a dealer, the price reflects the low mileage. This is the only Mustang visible in page-1 AutoTrader results; 4 more Fords exist in the broader 70-listing pool.
picks.whatToVerify: SN-95 convertible-specific rust: top-mount-to-rocker seam and floor drain area — inspect carefully in PA climate. 30-year-old rubber: check all hoses, belts, and brake lines regardless of mileage. Operate the soft top fully. T-45 transmission: 3rd gear synchro test (slow-shift to 3rd cold). Check rear axle for clunk on takeoff. Confirm original engine (low-mile cars sometimes have fresh motors with hidden history).
picks.bottomLine: 55K miles on a V8 Mustang convertible is the kind of find you don't walk away from — just inspect the rust-prone SN-95 convertible points thoroughly.
